records.ety: [AS. bolt; akin to Icel. bolti, Dan. bolt, D. bout, OHG. bolz, G. bolz, bolzen; of uncertain origin.]

defs: A shaft or missile intended to be shot from a crossbow or catapult, esp. a short, stout, blunt-headed arrow; a quarrel; an arrow, or that which resembles an arrow; a dart.
quotes: Look that the crossbowmen lack not bolts.
A fool's bolt is soon shot.

defs: Lightning; a thunderbolt.
A strong pin, of iron or other material, used to fasten or hold something in place, often having a head at one end and screw thread cut upon the other end.
A sliding catch, or fastening, as for a door or gate; the portion of a lock which is shot or withdrawn by the action of the key.
An iron to fasten the legs of a prisoner; a shackle; a fetter.
quotes: Away with him to prison! lay bolts enough upon him.

defs: A compact package or roll of cloth, as of canvas or silk, often containing about forty yards.
A bundle, as of oziers.
notes: See Tap bolt, Screw bolt, and Stud bolt.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Bolted; p. pr. & vb. n. Bolting.]

defs: To shoot; to discharge or drive forth.
To utter precipitately; to blurt or throw out.
quotes: I hate when Vice can bolt her arguments.

defs: To swallow without chewing; as, to bolt food; often used with down.
To refuse to support, as a nomination made by a party to which one has belonged or by a caucus in which one has taken part.
To cause to start or spring forth; to dislodge, as conies, rabbits, etc.
To fasten or secure with, or as with, a bolt or bolts, as a door, a timber, fetters; to shackle; to restrain.
quotes: Let tenfold iron bolt my door.
Which shackles accidents and bolts up change.

defs: To start forth like a bolt or arrow; to spring abruptly; to come or go suddenly; to dart; as, to bolt out of the room.
quotes: This Puck seems but a dreaming dolt, . . . And oft out of a bush doth bolt.

defs: To strike or fall suddenly like a bolt.
quotes: His cloudless thunder bolted on their heads.

defs: To spring suddenly aside, or out of the regular path; as, the horse bolted.
To refuse to support a nomination made by a party or a caucus with which one has been connected; to break away from a party.
In the manner of a bolt; suddenly; straight; unbendingly.
quotes: [He] came bolt up against the heavy dragoon.

defs: A sudden spring or start; a sudden spring aside; as, the horse made a bolt.
A sudden flight, as to escape creditors.
quotes: This gentleman was so hopelessly involved that he contemplated a bolt to America -- or anywhere.

defs: A refusal to support a nomination made by the party with which one has been connected; a breaking away from one's party.

records.ety: [OE. bolten, boulten, OF. buleter, F. bluter, fr. Ll. buletare, buratare, cf. F. bure coarse woolen stuff; fr. L. burrus red. See Borrel, and cf. Bultel.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Bolted; p. pr. & vb. n. Bolting.]

defs: To sift or separate the coarser from the finer particles of, as bran from flour, by means of a bolter; to separate, assort, refine, or purify by other means.
quotes: He now had bolted all the flour.

defs: To separate, as if by sifting or bolting; -- with out.
quotes: Time and nature will bolt out the truth of things.

defs: To discuss or argue privately, and for practice, as cases at law.
quotes: This bolts the matter fairly to the bran.
The report of the committee was examined and sifted and bolted to the bran.

defs: A sieve, esp. a long fine sieve used in milling for bolting flour and meal; a bolter.
